entries.name: DeepSeek-v4-Flash · vLLM @ 2× DGX Spark (1M ctx) — aidendle94 recipe
entries.summary: 2-Spark FP8 cluster recipe; 30-45 tok/s decode at 1M context

claims.body: Dead ends (per 0rand): FP4-Marlin backend incompatible with FP8 weights; manual PR-40082 build hits a FlashInfer/cutlass mismatch; standard spark-vllm-docker lacks b12x MoE (2× slower prefill). Requires `shm_size: 64gb` + `ulimits memlock=-1` or NCCL "unhandled system error". Launch worker node before head (~10s delay).

RECONCILIATION: num_speculative_tokens=2 — 0rand says "2 is optimal" vs a query about MTP=3 (no comparative metrics given). Replicated by stu.miller (35.6 t/s single / 63.9 t/s @c4) and MiaAI_Lab (40-45 t/s) on the same image — results agree within the concurrency-dependent band.

speed.note: @240K ctx, single request — see measurements for the conditions

recipe.command: dsv4-vllm-entrypoint serve deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Flash --tensor-parallel-size 2 --pipeline-parallel-size 1 --kv-cache-dtype fp8 --block-size 256 --max-model-len 1000000 --max-num-seqs 6 --max-num-batched-tokens 8192 --gpu-memory-utilization 0.82 --enable-prefix-caching --speculative-config '{"method":"mtp","num_speculative_tokens":2}' --distributed-executor-backend mp --enable-flashinfer-autotune --nnodes 2 --node-rank ${NODE_RANK} --master-addr ${MASTER_ADDR}

entries.name: DeepSeek-v4-Flash · DwarfStar (ds4) @ 1x DGX Spark (GB10, CUDA)
entries.summary: Single-Spark self-host via ds4 (CUDA) — cheaper alt to the 2-Spark vLLM cluster

claims.body: A single DGX Spark runs V4-Flash via ds4's CUDA backend — a cheaper single-box alternative to the 2-unit vLLM cluster recipe (ap-deepseek-v4-flash-2spark-vllm) on the same hardware class. SSD expert streaming lets one unit hold the model when unified memory is tight.

speed.note: single-Spark ds4 supported (CUDA backend); tok/s unbenchmarked in repo — measure

entries.name: DeepSeek-v4-Flash · DwarfStar (ds4) @ Mac (96/128GB, Metal)
entries.summary: Single-box Apple-Silicon self-host via ds4 q2-imatrix; routed-experts-only 2-bit

claims.body: Beta and model-coupled — runs only antirez-packaged V4 GGUFs. Quality impact of routed-experts-only 2-bit is unmeasured here vs the FP8 cluster recipe; the cluster path (ap-deepseek-v4-flash-2spark-vllm) has cited tool/IFEval numbers, this single-box path does not yet. Compare before choosing single-box convenience over cluster fidelity.

artifact.quant: q2-imatrix (routed experts only: up/gate IQ2_XXS, down Q2_K)

recipe.command: ds4  # interactive chat; sets ./ds4flash.gguf as default model pointer

entries.name: DeepSeek-v4-Flash · DwarfStar (ds4) @ Mac Studio M3 Ultra (512GB, q4)
entries.summary: Higher-fidelity q4 on a 512GB Mac Studio; ~same speed as q2, ~2x memory

claims.body: q4 vs q2 on the SAME M3 Ultra 512GB: near-identical speed (35.50 vs 36.86 t/s decode short) at higher fidelity and ~2x memory — a 512GB box can afford it. This is the catalog's quant tradeoff: a comparison across access-paths of one model, not duplicated model entries.

entries.name: DeepSeek-v4-Flash · OpenRouter (gateway)
entries.summary: Hosted access via OpenRouter — ~13x cheaper input than GLM-5.2

entries.name: DeepSeek-v4-PRO · DwarfStar (ds4) @ Mac Studio M3 Ultra (512GB, q2)
entries.summary: PRO on a 512GB Mac Studio via ds4; 9.56 t/s decode @ 32k ctx

claims.body: PRO is materially slower than Flash on the same box (9.56 vs 27-37 t/s decode) and needs a 512GB-class machine. PRO GGUF production still depends on the external llama.cpp workflow.

speed.note: from recipe comment ("~40 t/s generation in both single node and cluster"), not a benchmark. Recipe warns vLLM is suboptimal for this model even with the speed-optimization patch — slower than expected for the active-param count; cluster helps prompt processing, not generation.

    --max-num-seqs 64 \
    --gpu-memory-utilization 0.8 \
    -tp 1 \
    --host 0.0.0.0 \
    --port 8000

entries.name: Qwen3.5-122B · vLLM @ 1× Spark — Albond (current best)
entries.summary: AutoRound INT4 + Qwen FP8 shared-expert layers + MTP-1; 38.4 tok/s single-Spark

claims.body: Config progression (Albond): v1 AutoRound INT4 = 28.3 tok/s → v2 added Qwen FP8 shared-expert layers + MTP-1 = 38.4. Only the current-best config is a live entry; the full v1→v2 evolution is in this file's git history.
MTP-1 alone is the biggest win; the hybrid INT4+FP8 adds only ~2 tok/s on top of AutoRound+MTP (36.5 → 38.4).
Corroborated by norman.2 (~38.6-40 tok/s, same hybrid+MTP config, community vLLM docker).

quality.note: No output-quality measured for this AutoRound-INT4 config — INT4 quant CAN degrade outputs, so speed alone doesn't certify it. Reference ceiling = model-qwen35-122b published benchmarks; treat as UNVERIFIED for quality until an eval on THIS config exists.

speed.note: title claims 51 tok/s; author's VERIFIED single-Spark figure is 38.4

artifact.quant: hybrid INT4 (AutoRound) + FP8 (Qwen official, 144 shared-expert dense layers)

recipe.technique: MTP-1 speculative decoding (~95% accept); ~95-line vLLM INC quant patch

entries.name: Qwen3.5-122B · vLLM Ray cluster — AoE (contributor)
entries.summary: AutoRound INT4 on a Ray cluster; up to 56 tok/s — DISAGREES with single-Spark

claims.body: RECONCILIATION: results span 28.3 (v1) → 36.5 (AutoRound+MTP) → 38.4 (v2 hybrid, single Spark) → 56 (AoE Ray cluster). The title's "51 tok/s" sits between single-Spark v2 and the cluster — it is NOT the verified single-Spark number (38.4). Also: llama-benchy reports ~20 tok/s because it measures decode steps, not user-facing throughput — a metric disagreement, not a config one.

speed.note: Ray cluster — higher than single-Spark; explains the spread toward the title's 51

  --tensor-parallel-size 2 \
  --trust-remote-code \
  --kv-cache-dtype auto \
  --attention-backend flashinfer \
  --moe-backend marlin \
  --gpu-memory-utilization 0.4 \
  --max-model-len 262144 \
  --max-num-seqs 4 \
  --max-num-batched-tokens 8192 \
  --enable-chunked-prefill \
  --async-scheduling \
  --enable-prefix-caching \
  --speculative-config '{"method":"mtp","num_speculative_tokens":3,"moe_backend":"triton"}' \
  --load-format fastsafetensors \
  --reasoning-parser qwen3 \
  --tool-call-parser qwen3_xml \
  --enable-auto-tool-choice \
  --distributed-executor-backend ray

entries.name: Apple Silicon (unified memory)
entries.summary: M-series Mac with large unified memory (96/128GB+); MLX/Metal frameworks

claims.body: Single-box target for DwarfStar's DeepSeek-V4-Flash q2-imatrix path: a 96/128GB unified-memory Mac runs V4-Flash locally via Metal, contrasting the 2-unit DGX Spark vLLM cluster recipe for the same model.

entries.name: DGX Spark (GB10)
entries.summary: Unified-memory inference box; direct CUDA; clusterable; low power

relations.rationale: Direct NVIDIA CUDA support, easy clustering, ~60-80W draw.

claims.body: Two units (buy ASUS Ascent GX10 to save cost) can run DeepSeek-v4-Flash at 1M context and stay comfortably under USD 10k; ~60-80W per unit.

entries.name: AMD Strix Halo
entries.summary: APU-class unified-memory box; comparable price point to DGX Spark

entries.name: DeepSeek-v4-Flash
entries.summary: 284B/13B-active MoE; 1M context (YaRN); MLA + CSA/HCA hybrid attention; MIT

claims.body: VERIFIED against HF (2026-06-20): deepseek_v4 MoE, 284B total / 13B active, 43 layers, 256 routed experts + 1 shared, 6/token, MLA attention, native 65536 extended to 1M via YaRN (factor 16), MIT. Published weights are FP4 (MoE experts) + FP8 mixed. Replaces the earlier UNVERIFIED placeholder.

architecture.attention: MLA (64 heads, 1 KV head, head_dim 512) + CSA/HCA hybrid; sliding_window 128

quants.note: ds4 q2-imatrix — published to run on a 96/128GB box; consistent
the 2x DGX Spark (256GB) cluster recipe — relies on expert offload

provenance.source: estimated: total params x effective bits/8 (q2~2.8b q4~4.5b fp8~8b); cross-checked vs ds4 fit claims

entries.name: DeepSeek-v4-PRO
entries.summary: 1.6T/49B-active MoE; larger V4 sibling; 1M context (YaRN); MIT

claims.body: VERIFIED against HF (2026-06-20): deepseek_v4 MoE, 1.6T total / 49B active, 61 layers, 384 routed experts + 1 shared, 6/token, native 65536 extended to 1M via YaRN, MIT. Larger sibling of V4-Flash (not a quant/fine-tune of it). ds4 PRO GGUF production still depends on the external llama.cpp workflow.

quants.note: PRO q2 runs on a 512GB-class Mac Studio (measured 9.56 t/s)

provenance.source: estimated: total params x effective bits/8 (q2~2.8b q4~4.5b); cross-checked vs ds4 512GB fit

entries.name: Gemma 4 (family)
entries.summary: Google open-weights family; hybrid local-sliding + global attention

claims.body: Do NOT run any Gemma 4 GGUF on the CUDA 13.2 runtime — it produces poor outputs. Use a known-good CUDA build.

Disable thinking reliably with `llama-server` + the gemma4-native `enable_thinking:false`; `llama-cli` may not suppress thinking reliably. Do not feed prior thinking output back into multi-turn history.

architecture.notes: Global layers use unified K/V with Proportional RoPE (p-RoPE) for long-context memory.

entries.name: Gemma 4 26B-A4B (MoE)
entries.summary: Sparse MoE; ~4B active inference speed at 26B total; 256K context

entries.name: Gemma 4 31B (dense)
entries.summary: Largest dense variant; 256K context; strongest Gemma 4 benchmarks

entries.name: Gemma 4 e2b
entries.summary: On-device efficiency variant (Per-Layer Embeddings); fastest; small

claims.body: 128K context + 512-token sliding window are architectural HARD caps (PLE on-device design). `--ctx-size` above 131072 is silently clamped (HTTP 400 only on inference). Usable ~100K; not for long-context tasks.

entries.name: Gemma 4 e4b
entries.summary: On-device efficiency variant; barrier candidate; PLE

claims.body: 128K hard cap + 512 sliding window (same PLE limits as e2b).

entries.name: GLM-4.7-Flash
entries.summary: Fast MoE (glm4moelite arch); fast TTFT; cheap via gateways

claims.body: VERIFIED against HF (2026-06-20): glm4_moe_lite MoE, 31B total / 3B active (30B-A3B), 47 layers, 64 routed experts + 1 shared, 4 experts/token, 202752 native context (rope_scaling null), MIT. Corrects the earlier unverified "~14B" estimate.

entries.name: GLM-5.2
entries.summary: 753B MoE (glm_moe_dsa); MLA + DSA sparse attention; 1M context; MIT

claims.body: IndexShare: the DSA sparse-attention indexer is reused across every four sparse-attention layers (index_topk 2048), cutting per-token FLOPs ~2.9× at 1M context.
Active-parameter count is NOT stated for 5.2. The GLM-5 base scales to 744B total / 40B active — indicative only, not confirmed for 5.2.
Release ~2026-02-17 (inferred from arXiv id 2602.15763); not explicitly dated on the model card.

provenance.note: Terminal-Bench 2.1 is 81.0 in the z.ai blog comparison vs 82.7 on the model card — source variance flagged, model-card value used.

entries.name: GPT-OSS (family)
entries.summary: OpenAI open-weights MoE reasoning family; text-only; 128K context

claims.body: Jinja chat templates used by inference engines diverge from OpenAI's canonical Harmony format: tool-call args rendered with `tojson` backslash string symbols, extra newlines on turn boundaries, and tool-call thoughts must use `<|channel|>analysis` (not `final` — `final` is only for the last assistant message). Unsloth GGUFs have these fixed; prefer them for reliable tool calling.
Float16 causes infinities — the model was trained in BF16. Use BF16 or float32; float16 on T4/older GPUs (no native BF16) overflows after some time.

provenance.source: MODEL gpt-oss card (re-verify against primary source)

claims.body: MXFP4 is not natively supported on Ampere and older GPUs. Triton emulates it via `tl.dot_scaled` (upcasts to BF16 internally) — works but slower than native hardware support (Blackwell+).

provenance.source: MODEL gpt-oss card (re-verify against primary source)

claims.body: Quantized weights below F16 (incl. 2-bit) reportedly show minimal accuracy loss — attention layers stay higher precision while MoE routing layers are compressed. The `hf-gpt-oss:120b-q3` hub build is broken (1/5 quality on the card's note); use Q4 or above.
Ollama compatibility is not confirmed by the card; Unsloth recommends llama.cpp backends. (Referenced runtimes: runtime-llamacpp, runtime-ollama.)

Unsloth Flex Attention enables long-context fine-tuning on a single 80GB H100: ~60K ctx BF16 LoRA / ~81K ctx QLoRA, vs ~9K standard and ~15K with FA3. FA3 lacks the backward pass for the sink mechanism — do NOT use FA3 for gpt-oss training.

GAP: the card cites no vendor-published benchmark numbers — only comparative positioning (120b "rivals o4-mini", 20b "rivals o3-mini"; both outperform o1 and GPT-4o on function calling / CoT). Re-verify quantitative benchmarks against OpenAI's primary model card before relying on them.

provenance.source: MODEL gpt-oss card (re-verify against primary source)

architecture.notes: A single learnable sink token is appended to each attention computation to prevent first-token collapse in the sliding-window layers. RL-trained reasoning models; English-primary but multilingual capable.

entries.name: GPT-OSS 120B
entries.summary: Larger MoE variant; rivals o4-mini; locally runnable (~66GB 1-bit)

architecture.notes: Comparative positioning: rivals o4-mini; strong function calling and CoT reasoning. Approx. on-disk size ~66GB at 1-bit / ~74GB at Q4. The card warns the `hf-gpt-oss:120b-q3` hub build is broken — use Q4 or above.

entries.name: GPT-OSS 20B
entries.summary: Smaller MoE variant; rivals o3-mini; locally runnable (~14GB Q4)

architecture.notes: Comparative positioning: rivals o3-mini; strong function calling and CoT reasoning. Approx. on-disk size ~14GB at dynamic Q4 / ~20GB at F16; 2-bit (~11.5GB) reportedly near-identical to F16 (~14GB) per the card.

entries.name: MiniMax-M2 (family)
entries.summary: MiniMax open-weights MoE (minimax_m2); 256-expert/8-active; MTP; 192K context

claims.body: VERIFIED against HF config.json (2026-06-20): model_type minimax_m2, 62 layers, 256 routed experts, 8 experts/token, hidden 3072, attn_type_list all full attention, partial RoPE (rotary_dim 64, rope_theta 5e6), MTP (num_mtp_modules 3), 196608 native context, vocab 200064. Total/active params (~230B/~10B) estimated from the BF16 safetensors index — re-verify against a primary published count.

entries.name: MiniMax-M2.5
entries.summary: Later-generation MiniMax-M2 (same minimax_m2 arch); distinct weights

entries.name: MiniMax-M2.7
entries.summary: Later-generation MiniMax-M2 (same minimax_m2 arch); distinct weights

entries.name: Nemotron (family)
entries.summary: NVIDIA open-weights family; nemotron_h Mamba2-Transformer hybrid; 256K context

claims.body: CORRECTION: the source card's "NoPE / no positional embeddings / 1M context" description was NOT supported by the canonical config.json — real model_type is nemotron_h (Mamba2-Transformer hybrid), context 256K. Do not rely on a 1M native context for this family.

provenance.source: nvidia/* HF config.json vs MODEL nemotron card

claims.body: Thinking is always on: no confirmed `--reasoning-budget` suppression — thinking is structural (`<think>` tokens in the template). Strip `<think>...</think>` blocks in post-processing if clean output is needed.

provenance.source: MODEL nemotron card (re-verify against primary source)

claims.body: Ollama `think` flag unreliable for this family — use llama.cpp for reliable thinking behaviour.

provenance.source: MODEL nemotron card (re-verify against primary source)

claims.body: No vendor-published benchmark numbers in the source card — benchmark facets omitted pending a primary source.

architecture.notes: Hybrid state-space (Mamba2) + Transformer layers. Designed for coding, math, agentic, reasoning.

provenance.source: nvidia/* HF config.json (verified: model_type nemotron_h, max_position_embeddings 262144, vocab 131072)

thinking.disable: no --reasoning-budget support confirmed; strip <think>...</think> in post-processing if needed

provenance.source: MODEL nemotron card (re-verify against primary source)

entries.name: Nemotron Cascade-2 30B-A3B
entries.summary: Cascade-2; post-trained from Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B-Base; MoE 3B active

entries.name: Nemotron 3 Nano 30B-A3B (MoE)
entries.summary: Sparse MoE; 3B active at ~31B total; 256K context

entries.name: Nemotron 3 Nano 4B
entries.summary: Smallest variant; DENSE ~4B (no experts); 256K context

entries.name: Nemotron 3 Super 120B-A12B (MoE)
entries.summary: Largest variant; sparse MoE; 12B active at 120B total; 256K context

claims.body: Fine-tuning: BF16 LoRA ~256GB VRAM (multi-GPU); router-layer FT disabled by default for stability; QLoRA is the practical single-node option.

provenance.source: MODEL nemotron card (re-verify against primary source)

entries.name: Nemotron 3 Ultra 550B-A55B (MoE)
entries.summary: Flagship Nemotron-3; sparse MoE; 55B active at 550B total; nemotron_h hybrid

claims.body: Params (550B total / 55B active) and 256K context taken from the repo name + the nemotron_h family config (Mamba2-Transformer hybrid); the Ultra config.json was not independently fetched, so treat layer/expert counts as unverified for this variant (architecture inherited from the family).

entries.name: Qwen3-Coder-Next
entries.summary: Alibaba/Qwen coding-optimised MoE; ~3B active of ~80B; non-reasoning instruct; 256K context

claims.body: `--min-p 0.01` is required — llama.cpp defaults to 0.05, which diverges from the Qwen-recommended sampling.
Historical llama.cpp `vectorized key_gdiff` bug (fixed 2026-02-04) caused looping and bad outputs. Re-download GGUFs and update llama.cpp if they were downloaded before this date.

provenance.source: MODEL qwen3coder card (re-verify against primary source)

claims.body: Tool-calling parsing improved in llama.cpp on 2026-02-19; update llama.cpp for best agentic/tool-use results.

provenance.source: MODEL qwen3coder card (re-verify against primary source)

claims.body: The HuggingFace `hf-qwen3-coder-next` build pulls a thinking variant — `think:false` is ignored in some versions. Use the Unsloth GGUF for reliable no-think behaviour.
Q8 multi-shard registration is broken in Ollama 0.20.4 (bad registration). Use Q4_K_M or serve via llama.cpp directly.

provenance.source: MODEL qwen3coder card (re-verify against primary source)

claims.body: Fine-tuning fits on a single B200 GPU for BF16 LoRA via Unsloth.
GAP: the card publishes no vendor benchmark numbers (MMLU, LiveCodeBench, etc.) for Qwen3-Coder-Next — benchmarks facet omitted. Re-mine the HuggingFace model card if published figures are needed.

architecture.notes: Non-reasoning instruct — no thinking mode. Designed for long-horizon agentic coding, complex tool use, and recovery from execution failures. Qwen claims comparable performance to models with 10–20x more active parameters.

entries.name: Qwen3.5 (family)
entries.summary: Hybrid Gated-DeltaNet + attention; thinking; multimodal; 201 languages

claims.body: DeltaNet linear-attention majority → lower KV memory and higher throughput than an equivalent-size transformer; better long-context memory profile.

Quant guidance (DeltaNet-hybrid tensor sensitivity): `ssm_out` very sensitive (degrades sharply below Q4); `attn_*` sensitive (keep Q4+); `ffn_up`/`gate_exps` tolerate 3-bit. Recommended UD-Q4_K_XL / UD-Q4_K_M. MXFP4 retired (worse than Q4_K on many tensors). Prefer task evals over perplexity (PPL is not a chat/code-quality proxy).

provenance.source: Unsloth GGUF analysis (via MODEL qwen35 card)

claims.body: Ollama incompatible (separate mmproj vision files); use llama.cpp. Think flag unreliable in Ollama — use `--reasoning-budget 0` or `--chat-template-kwargs '{"enable_thinking":false}'`. With thinking on, answers land in `choices[0].message.reasoning_content` (not `content`) — clients must read both.

provenance.source: MODEL qwen35 card (re-verify against primary source)

claims.body: License not stated in the source card — needs a primary source (HF model card).

architecture.notes: Most layers are Gated DeltaNet (linear attention, O(n)); ~1-in-4 full attention. Trained with Multi-Token Prediction (MTP).

entries.name: Qwen3.5-122B-A10B
entries.summary: Larger MoE sibling; same hybrid-deltanet layout; 256K context

entries.name: Qwen3.5-27B (dense)
entries.summary: Dense; 64 layers (deep for its size); competitive with 35B-A3B

entries.name: Qwen3.5-35B-A3B
entries.summary: MoE; leads TAU2 agentic for its size (beats the 122B); strong general

claims.body: Qwen3.5-Flash is the hosted form of 35B-A3B (1M context, built-in tools).

entries.name: Qwen3.5-397B-A17B
entries.summary: Flagship MoE; hosted as Qwen3.5-Plus (1M ctx); not locally practical

claims.body: Hosted as Qwen3.5-Plus (1M context, built-in tools); ~200GB+ — multi-node for local.

entries.name: Qwen3.5-9B (dense)
entries.summary: Dense; ~6GB Q4; 256K context with DeltaNet — small-footprint candidate

entries.name: Qwen 3.6 (family)
entries.summary: Alibaba open-weights multimodal family; hybrid Gated DeltaNet + Attention MoE

claims.body: Thinking on by default (`<think>...\n\n` before response). Disable with the qwen36-native `enable_thinking:false`; the soft-switch `/think` `/nothink` tokens are NOT supported (unlike Qwen3). `preserve_thinking` retains reasoning traces from prior turns to reduce redundant re-reasoning in agent sessions and improve KV cache utilisation; minimum recommended context to preserve thinking is 128K tokens.

Ollama incompatible — separate mmproj vision files are not handled; use llama.cpp backends. Gibberish output can occur if context is too low; try `--cache-type-k bf16 --cache-type-v bf16`. 128K minimum context recommended to preserve thinking capability.

`reasoning_content` split (llama.cpp OpenAI-compatible API, thinking enabled): thinking tokens land in `choices[0]["message"]["reasoning_content"]`, NOT `content`. For complex prompts `content` may be empty — the answer must be read from `reasoning_content`. Family-wide (all Qwen3.5/3.6 thinking models, not version-specific). Client code must check both fields. The inline `<think>` tag is what the Jinja template generates internally; the llama.cpp API splits it into the two fields.

provenance.source: MODEL qwen36 card (re-verify against primary source)

claims.body: Unsloth GGUFs support a `developer` role for Codex/OpenCode agentic tools. Qwen 3.6 adds improved agentic coding (frontend workflows, repo-level reasoning) vs Qwen3.5.

architecture.notes: Identical architecture to Qwen3.5. Layer pattern: 10 × (3 × (Gated DeltaNet → MoE) → 1 × (Gated Attention → MoE)). MTP (multi-token prediction) present. Vision encoder present — requires `--mmproj` for multimodal in llama.cpp; text-only omits `--mmproj` (or vLLM `--language-model-only`).

thinking.note: No `/think` `/nothink` soft-switch tokens (unlike Qwen3). Use chat-template-kwargs or reasoning-budget instead.

sampling.presence_penalty: 0–2 to reduce repetitions; higher may slightly cost quality

entries.name: Qwen 3.6 35B-A3B (MoE)
entries.summary: Sparse MoE; 3B active at 35B total; 262K native (→1M YaRN); multimodal

claims.body: 262K native context, extensible to ~1M via YaRN. Approximate memory footprint by quant (vendor/community estimate): 3-bit ~17 GB, 4-bit ~23 GB, 6-bit ~30 GB, 8-bit ~38 GB, BF16 ~70 GB.

Only the 35B-A3B variant has been released (2026-04-16); additional Qwen 3.6 variants are expected but not yet published. Vendor-published benchmarks above are notably stronger on coding-agent tasks vs Qwen3.5-35B-A3B (SWE-bench Verified +3.4, Terminal-Bench +11, QwenWebBench +43%); general knowledge/math (MMLU-Pro, GPQA, AIME) is comparable to 3.5.

entries.name: Step-3.7-Flash
entries.summary: StepFun multimodal MoE (step3p7); 288-expert/8-active text MoE + vision encoder; 256K context

claims.body: VERIFIED against HF config.json (2026-06-20): model_type step3p7 (text backbone step3p5 + perception_encoder vision tower), 45 text layers, 288 routed experts, top-8/token, 1 shared expert (share_expert_dim 1280), hidden 4096, GQA 64 heads / 8 groups, vocab 128896, native context 262144 (llama3 RoPE scaling factor 2.0 over 131072 original, YaRN on full-attention layers), apache-2.0. Total params (~200B) estimated from the BF16 index; active-param count not published — re-verify against a primary source.

entries.name: Anthropic API
entries.summary: First-party API for Claude models (proprietary)

entries.name: Amazon Bedrock Mantle
entries.summary: OpenAI-spec endpoint fronting many non-GPT models (GLM, Nemotron, …)

claims.body: Reported to run agent workloads on cheap models (e.g. z.ai GLM) at ~1/10th the cost of first-party Sonnet, with acceptable quality for those tasks.

compatCaveats: Some models may not support every feature of the OpenAI API surface.

entries.name: DeepSeek API
entries.summary: First-party API for DeepSeek models (also open-weights)

entries.name: Google AI / Gemini API
entries.summary: First-party API for Gemini (proprietary) + Gemma (open)

entries.name: OpenAI API
entries.summary: First-party API for GPT / o-series models (proprietary)

entries.name: OpenRouter
entries.summary: Aggregator proxying many models from many upstreams via one OpenAI-compatible API

claims.body: Routes each model to one or more upstream sub-providers; the SAME model may carry multiple price/latency tiers (e.g. :free, :nitro, by upstream). Prices change frequently — sync from the /models endpoint, don't pin.

entries.name: z.ai API
entries.summary: First-party API for GLM models (also open-weights on HF)

entries.name: DwarfStar (ds4)
entries.summary: Model-coupled self-host engine for DeepSeek-V4 Flash/PRO; Metal/CUDA/ROCm

claims.body: Model-coupled and NOT a generic GGUF runner: loads only antirez-packaged DeepSeek-V4 layouts from huggingface.co/antirez/deepseek-v4-gguf (the `facets.formats: [gguf]` here is the container, not loadability of generic GGUFs). PRO GGUF production still depends on the external llama.cpp-based workflow; native conversion tooling "can be added later".

Notable harness ideas distinct from llama.cpp/vLLM: SSD streaming of routed MoE experts (RAM budget caps resident experts), on-disk `.kv` session cache keyed by SHA1 of the rendered byte prefix (`/save` `/list` `/switch` `/del`), and exact-DSML tool-call replay for stateless API sessions (server maps an unguessable tool id → the exact sampled DSML block so re-rendered prompts stay byte-identical and the KV checkpoint never misaligns). Also: MTP spec-decode, directional steering, distributed inference, OpenAI/Anthropic-compatible REST.

Beta, GPT-5.5-assisted, "exists only for a few days"; antirez notes the target model family may change as the landscape evolves. Treat all ds4 entries as low-confidence and fast-decaying — re-verify against the repo.

entries.name: llama.cpp
entries.summary: Widely-used self-host GGUF inference engine (llama-server)

claims.body: Thinking control: `--reasoning-budget 0` suppresses reasoning, `-1` = unlimited. With thinking enabled the server splits reasoning into `choices[0].message.reasoning_content` (not `content`) — clients must read both; `content` can be empty on complex prompts.

entries.name: mlx-lm
entries.summary: Apple-Silicon inference engine built on the MLX framework; loads MLX-format weights

claims.body: mlx-lm is the ENGINE (the `pip install mlx-lm` package, with an OpenAI-compatible `mlx_lm.server`); "MLX" itself is Apple's acceleration FRAMEWORK (peer of CUDA/ROCm), and `mlx` is the weight FORMAT. The format is a third ecosystem alongside GGUF (llama.cpp/ollama) and safetensors (vLLM/SGLang): it runs only via mlx-lm / LM Studio on Apple Silicon, so choosing it constrains both the engine AND the hardware. NOT "metal" — Metal is the generic GPU API beneath everything Apple.

entries.name: Ollama
entries.summary: Self-host engine with a model hub and templated GGUFs

claims.body: `num_predict` does not cap thinking tokens, so thinking models can hang indefinitely with `stream:false`. Prefer llama.cpp `--reasoning-budget` for reliable think/nothink control on reasoning models.

entries.name: spark-vllm-docker (eugr)
entries.summary: Containerised vLLM build + cluster orchestration for DGX Spark (GB10)

claims.body: Reproducible vLLM-on-GB10 distribution: base nvidia/cuda 13.0.2-devel-ubuntu24.04 (arch 12.1a), FlashInfer + fastsafetensors + InstantTensor loading, a "mods" system for model-specific patches without rebuild, non-privileged container w/ RDMA, prebuilt vLLM/FlashInfer wheels. `./launch-cluster.sh [--solo] exec vllm serve <model> …`; multi-node auto-discovery over IB/RDMA (NCCL). Ships recipes for Qwen3.5/ 3.6, GLM-4.7, Gemma4, Nemotron, MiniMax-M2, gpt-oss, Step. No registry image — built locally via build-and-copy.sh (tag `vllm-node`). An alternative to the forum's aidendle94/sparkrun-vllm image. The engine is vLLM (served-by); this entry is the packaged build (built-with).

entries.name: vLLM
entries.summary: High-throughput serving engine; tensor/pipeline parallel, FP8 KV, spec decoding

claims.body: Supports tensor-parallel + multi-node, MTP speculative decoding, FP8 KV cache, prefix caching. Preferred over llama.cpp for production throughput and multi-GPU/cluster; the published DGX Spark recipes (forum 365639, 372268) are vLLM-based.

entries.name: Intel AutoRound
entries.summary: Sign-gradient-descent rounding PTQ; strong INT4; safetensors

claims.body: SignRound-based PTQ; competitive/strong INT4 checkpoints. safetensors; vLLM. Used by the Qwen3.5-122B single-Spark recipe (Intel AutoRound INT4).

entries.name: AWQ
entries.summary: Activation-aware weight quant — protects salient weights; safetensors

claims.body: Activation-aware Weight Quantization: scales/protects the most salient weight channels, typically beating GPTQ at 4-bit quality. safetensors; vLLM / transformers / SGLang.

entries.name: Context extension (RoPE scaling / YaRN)
entries.summary: Run past native context via RoPE-scaling/YaRN; lossy at the extended tail

claims.body: Extending past nativeContext (RoPE scaling / YaRN, or vLLM `--max-model-len` + `VLLM_ALLOW_LONG_MAX_MODEL_LEN`) trades long-range fidelity — quality can degrade at the extended tail ("lost in the middle"). NOT all models extend: some hard-cap (Gemma e2b clamps to 128k). Verify long-context quality at the target length, don't assume.

entries.name: DFlash speculative decoding
entries.summary: Separate DFlash draft model for speculative decoding (Qwen3.6); LOSSLESS

claims.body: DFlash uses a separate small draft model (z-lab/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-DFlash) to speculate tokens that the base model verifies — lossless, a throughput win. vLLM `--speculative-config '{"method":"dflash","model":...,"num_speculative_tokens":15}'`. Used by the Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-FP8 dflash recipe.

entries.name: Expert parallelism (EP)
entries.summary: Shard MoE experts across GPUs; pairs with TP for large MoE

claims.body: Distributes MoE experts across devices (only routed experts compute per token); pairs with TP for large MoE. Lossless.

entries.name: FlashAttention / FlashInfer
entries.summary: Fused exact attention kernel — faster + lower memory; LOSSLESS

claims.body: IO-aware fused attention (FlashAttention-2/3, FlashInfer) — cuts attention memory and speeds prefill/decode; mathematically EXACT (no quality change). The DeepSeek-v4-Flash recipe enables FlashInfer autotune.

entries.name: FP8 (8-bit float)
entries.summary: Near-lossless 8-bit float; native on Hopper/Blackwell; safetensors

claims.body: 8-bit floating point (E4M3/E5M2), near-lossless vs BF16 and hardware-native on Hopper/Blackwell — minimal quality loss for good throughput. safetensors; vLLM. Used by the DeepSeek-v4-Flash recipe (official FP8 weights + FP8 KV).

provenance.source: FP8 / vLLM quantization docs + NVIDIA forum 372268

entries.name: GGUF k-quant
entries.summary: Standard llama.cpp k-quant GGUF compression (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, Q8_0, …)

claims.body: Perplexity / KLD (calibrated on wiki text) is NOT a reliable proxy for chat or code quality — a quant that scores well on PPL can produce worse outputs. Prefer task-based evals over published PPL.

provenance.source: Unsloth GGUF analysis (via MODEL qwen35 card)

entries.name: GPTQ
entries.summary: Calibration-based post-training weight quant (INT4/INT3); safetensors

claims.body: Layer-wise calibration PTQ, commonly INT4. Ships in safetensors; loaded by vLLM / transformers / SGLang (NOT llama.cpp). Mature; AWQ often edges it on 4-bit quality.

entries.name: KV eviction / compression (SnapKV, H2O, StreamingLLM)
entries.summary: Drop/compress less-important KV tokens — LOSSY at long context

claims.body: SnapKV / H2O / StreamingLLM evict or compress KV entries to fit long context in less memory — trades long-range coherence for footprint. A config-QUALITY factor: measure long-context quality before relying on it.

entries.name: KV-cache quant (bf16)
entries.summary: Lower KV memory / fix gibberish via --cache-type-k/v bf16 (llama.cpp)

claims.body: Gibberish output at low context or large KV — try `--cache-type-k bf16 --cache-type-v bf16`.

entries.name: 2-bit routed-MoE-expert quant (asymmetric)
entries.summary: Quantize ONLY routed MoE experts (up/gate IQ2_XXS, down Q2_K); leave the rest full-precision

claims.body: DwarfStar's DeepSeek-V4 quant: only the routed MoE experts are quantized (up/gate at IQ2_XXS, down at Q2_K) — the majority of model space — while shared experts, projections and routing are left untouched to preserve quality. Aggressive-where-cheap, exact-where-it-matters; the foundation for fitting V4-Flash on a 96/128GB unified-memory Mac.

entries.name: Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) speculative decoding
entries.summary: Model-native MTP draft head for speculative decoding; LOSSLESS (verified accepts)

claims.body: MTP uses the model's own multi-token-prediction head(s) to draft several tokens per step, verified by the base model — accepted tokens are identical to standard decoding, so it is lossless (pure throughput win). vLLM `--speculative-config '{"method":"mtp","num_speculative_tokens":N}'`. Used by the Qwen3.6-NVFP4, Nemotron-3-Ultra and DeepSeek-V4 recipes.

entries.name: MXFP4 (microscaling 4-bit float)
entries.summary: OCP microscaling 4-bit float weight quant; native on Blackwell; safetensors

claims.body: MXFP4 is the OCP microscaling 4-bit float format (shared block scale). gpt-oss ships MXFP4 MoE weights; vLLM serves them via FlashInfer/CUTLASS MXFP4 backends. 4-bit → lossy, but the gpt-oss card reports attention layers stay higher precision while MoE routing is compressed, so accuracy loss is minimal. Native on Blackwell; emulated (slower) on older GPUs.

entries.name: NVFP4 (NVIDIA 4-bit float)
entries.summary: NVIDIA 4-bit float (FP4) weight quant; Blackwell-native; safetensors

claims.body: NVFP4 is NVIDIA's 4-bit floating-point weight format (block-scaled FP4), designed to run on Blackwell-class hardware (GB10/GB200). 4-bit → lossy vs BF16/FP8, smaller footprint for good throughput. Ships in safetensors; loaded by vLLM (Marlin / CUTLASS MoE backends on GB10). The eugr spark-vllm-docker recipes serve Nemotron-3 / Qwen3.6 / Step-3.7 NVFP4 checkpoints.

entries.name: PagedAttention
entries.summary: KV-cache memory paging (vLLM) — eliminates fragmentation; LOSSLESS

claims.body: Virtual-memory-style paging of the KV cache: near-zero fragmentation, enables high concurrency + long context in less memory. Mathematically exact — pure throughput/memory win, no quality change. vLLM core.

entries.name: Pipeline parallelism (PP)
entries.summary: Split model layers across GPUs/nodes (inter-layer); tolerates slower links

claims.body: Assigns layer ranges to different devices/nodes — far less interconnect-sensitive than TP, so it suits slower/multi-node links, at some pipeline-bubble latency cost. vLLM `--pipeline-parallel-size`. Lossless.

entries.name: Prefix caching
entries.summary: Reuse KV for shared prompt prefixes (system/few-shot); LOSSLESS

claims.body: Caches and reuses KV for repeated prompt prefixes — large TTFT/throughput win on shared context (system prompts, agents). Exact. DeepSeek recipe: `--enable-prefix-caching`.

entries.name: Ray / data parallelism
entries.summary: Replicate the model across nodes for throughput (multi-replica)

claims.body: Ray-orchestrated multi-replica/multi-node serving for concurrency/throughput (vs TP/PP which split one replica). The AoE Qwen3.5-122B 56 tok/s result used a Ray cluster. Lossless.

entries.name: SSD expert streaming
entries.summary: Stream routed MoE experts from SSD on cache miss; RAM budget caps resident experts

claims.body: Run models larger than RAM by keeping only a budget of complete routed experts resident and loading the rest from SSD on miss (`--ssd-streaming --ssd-streaming-cache-experts 32GB`; auto budget = 80% of the Metal recommended working set minus non-routed weights). Distinct from KV eviction/compression (technique-kv-eviction): this streams WEIGHTS, not the KV cache. Lossless quality; trades decode latency on miss.

entries.name: Tensor parallelism (TP)
entries.summary: Shard each layer's tensors across GPUs (intra-layer); needs fast interconnect

claims.body: Splits attention/FFN matrices across N GPUs — lowers per-GPU memory and speeds compute, but is interconnect-bound (NVLink / RoCE). vLLM `--tensor-parallel-size`. The DeepSeek 2-Spark recipe uses TP=2 over ConnectX-7. Lossless.

entries.name: Unsloth Dynamic GGUF (UD-Q*)
entries.summary: Per-tensor dynamic bit allocation protecting sensitive tensors; imatrix

claims.body: UD-Q4_K_XL / UD-Q4_K_M protect quant-sensitive tensors (keep them at higher bit depth) while letting tolerant tensors go lower — better quality than flat k-quant at the same nominal bit depth. imatrix variants ~5–10% slower but higher quality at a given bit depth (worth it for long-context / precise tasks). MXFP4 retired from the Dynamic lineup (worse than Q4_K on many tensors; native-hardware only).

provenance.source: Unsloth GGUF analysis (via MODEL qwen35 card)
